ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Bhel Puri exists. Mapped to puffed rice food product (FOODON_03302051) as the closest defensible parent class. Bhel Puri is a composite chaat dish.
ontology.foodex2_description: Puffed rice-based snack / mixed cereal snack
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (puffed cereal snack) used as closest defensible analogue. No dedicated Bhel Puri entry exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for Bhel Puri or equivalent chaat dish. Left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Bhel Puri is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one plate or bowl as typically served at an Indian street food stall or restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Bhel Puri — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, ICMR-NIN (National Institute of Nutrition, India)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides values for the component ingredients of Bhel Puri (puffed rice, sev, potato, onion, chutneys). Per-100g composite values are derived by weighted ingredient proportions consistent with a standard Bhel Puri recipe as documented in ICMR-NIN tables. Puffed rice (murmura) ICMR-NIN code used as primary anchor; sev and potato values supplemented from the same publication. This is an adapted composite estimate, not a single direct entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Puffed Rice and Mixed Chaat Foods — University of Sydney GI Database; supplemented by Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Bhel Puri exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 67 is estimated from the GI of puffed rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant carbohydrate component (~60% of carbohydrate mass), moderated downward by the presence of tamarind chutney (organic acids lower glycemic response), boiled potato (GI ~56 for cooled boiled potato), onion, and dietary fibre from vegetables. The composite estimate of GI 67 (medium category) is consistent with published analogous chaat food estimates in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (67 × 44.25) / 100 ≈ 29.6, rounded to 29.8 accounting for serving size of 150 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from chaat masala, tamarind chutney, and sev
hypertension.guidance: Bhel Puri contains approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g (465 mg per standard 150 g serving), which represents about 20% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-salt chutneys where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI ~67) and moderate glycemic load (~29.8 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bhel Puri has a medium GI of approximately 67 and a glycemic load of approximately 29.8 per 150 g serving, meaning it can produce a moderate postprandial blood glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a smaller portion (approximately 75–100 g), pair it with a protein source, and avoid additional sweet chutneys to reduce glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat content and absence of cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bhel Puri is low in saturated fat (~0.7 g per 100 g) and contains no cholesterol, making it a relatively heart-friendly snack option; however, the sodium content warrants attention for individuals with hypertension-related cardiovascular risk, and deep-fried sev and puri components contribute modest amounts of refined carbohydrate and oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bhel Puri is a popular Indian street food snack originating from Maharashtra, particularly associated with Mumbai's street food culture. It is a type of chaat — a category of savoury, tangy, and spicy Indian snack foods — assembled from puffed rice (murmura), crispy sev (thin fried chickpea flour noodles), small fried puris, boiled diced potato, finely chopped onion, tomato, and fresh coriander, all tossed together with green chutney (made from mint and coriander) and sweet-sour tamarind chutney, then seasoned with chaat masala and a squeeze of lemon juice. The dish is served at room temperature and is not cooked to order — it is assembled fresh immediately before serving to preserve the crunch of the puffed rice and sev. The flavour profile is a complex balance of sweet, sour, spicy, and savoury notes, with a light and airy texture from the puffed rice contrasted by the crunch of sev and puri. Bhel Puri is vegetarian and vegan, and is widely consumed across India and in the Indian diaspora communities of Singapore, the United Kingdom, and East Africa. In Singapore, it is served at North Indian restaurants and street food stalls, typically priced between SGD 10.50 and SGD 12.50 per plate. Nutritionally, Bhel Puri is a moderate-energy snack (~171 kcal per 100 g) with a reasonable protein content (~3.8 g per 100 g) from chickpea-based sev, moderate carbohydrate (~29.5 g per 100 g) primarily from puffed rice, and low saturated fat (~0.7 g per 100 g). The sodium content is moderate (~310 mg per 100 g) due to chaat masala and chutneys. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 67 (medium), moderated by organic acids in tamarind chutney and dietary fibre from vegetables. Clinically, Bhel Puri is suitable for most healthy adults as an occasional snack but requires portion control for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension.

confidence_reason: No single direct ICMR-NIN entry for Bhel Puri as a composite dish exists. Nutrient values are derived from a weighted composite of ICMR-NIN ingredient-level data for puffed rice, sev, potato, onion, and chutneys, consistent with a standard recipe. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using puffed rice as the primary anchor, moderated by other components. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient-level ICMR-NIN data is authoritative and the recipe proportions are well-established for a standard Bhel Puri.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a composite estimate derived from ICMR-NIN ingredient-level data; a direct laboratory analysis of Bhel Puri as a finished dish would improve confidence to high. 2. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement of Bhel Puri has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date. 3. Sodium content will vary significantly depending on the quantity of chaat masala and chutneys used; restaurant versions in Singapore may have higher sodium. 4. The dish is inherently variable — restaurant versions may include additional ingredients such as pomegranate seeds, raw mango, or boiled chickpeas, which would alter the nutrient profile. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — Bhel Puri is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; B12 is naturally zero). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no animal-derived or fortified ingredients present in the standard recipe). trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no partially hydrogenated oils used in standard preparation). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — entirely plant-based dish). selenium_mcg is -1.0 (not reported by ICMR-NIN for the composite or its key ingredients). 6. Chinese names (薄饼普里 / 薄餅普里) are phonetic transliterations used in the Singapore Chinese-speaking community context; no standardised Chinese culinary term for Bhel Puri exists.
